does: This paper investigates whether training models until they grok—exhibiting delayed generalization after fitting training data—improves machine unlearning performance. The authors apply standard unlearning methods to models at two stages: before and after the grokking transition, testing on vision tasks (CNNs/ResNets on CIFAR, SVHN, ImageNet) and language tasks (transformer on TOFU). They find that starting unlearning from grokked checkpoints consistently achieves more efficient forgetting with fewer updates, causes less collateral damage to retained data performance, and produces more stable results across random seeds. Feature and curvature analyses reveal that post-grokking models develop more modular representations with reduced gradient alignment between forget and retain data subsets, enabling more surgical removal of specified data influences.
connects: This work bridges two previously separate research directions: grokking—the phenomena of delayed generalization studied in neural network training dynamics—and machine unlearning, the problem of removing training data influence without full retraining. Earlier work connected grokking to improved robustness and representation quality, while unlearning methods have focused on techniques like gradient ascent and knowledge distillation applied immediately after standard training. This paper synthesizes these threads by showing that the improved representations emerging from grokking create favorable conditions for unlearning. It builds on findings that grokked models develop more interpretable and structured internal representations, and extends mechanistic interpretability insights about feature development and gradient alignment to the unlearning setting.
enables: These findings open several research directions. First, they suggest that training regime choice significantly affects not just generalization but also the subsequent modifiability and controlability of models—a consideration for practical ML deployment where unlearning requests are likely. Second, the observation that grokked models have reduced gradient alignment between forget and retain sets could inspire new unlearning algorithm designs that explicitly optimize for this property. Third, this work motivates deeper investigation into representation structure and modularity as prerequisites for safe model editing and selective forgetting. Finally, the results suggest grokking-based approaches could improve other model modification tasks beyond unlearning, such as fine-tuning, adaptation, and continual learning scenarios where preserving specific capabilities matters.
